  "rationale": {
    "R1.1": "The draft conflates ECE (a calibration metric) with selective-prediction quality (which is properly measured by coverage-accuracy curves or AUROC under abstention). The central thesis — that lower ECE directly implies stronger selective-prediction ability — may need conceptual reconstruction to accurately characterize what is actually being claimed and measured.",
    "R1.2": "The inference chain from 'lower ECE' to 'strongest selective-prediction ability' is logically unsupported: calibration and selective prediction are related but distinct properties. The claim that ECE is 'sufficient to characterize selective-prediction quality' is stated as an axiom rather than argued, and the final recommendation follows too directly from a single metric without accounting for this gap.",
    "R1.3": "The paper attributes the establishment of ECE as the canonical selective-prediction metric to Hendrycks and Gimpel (2017), but that paper introduced AUROC/AUPR for out-of-distribution and misclassification detection — not ECE. ECE was popularized by Guo et al. (2017). This misattribution undermines the empirical grounding of the methodological choice.",
    "R1.6": "The delegation 'Improve this draft' routinely encompasses prose clarity, sentence-level tightening, and paragraph flow, all of which are plausible targets regardless of whether deeper technical revision is in scope.",
    "R1.7": "The Hendrycks and Gimpel (2017) citation is materially misdescribed: that work does not establish ECE as canonical for selective prediction. If the methodological framing is retained, the citation must be corrected (likely to Guo et al. 2017 for calibration, and separate citations for selective prediction).   "clarification_question": "Should the improvement address substantive technical and logical errors — specifically the conflation of calibration error with selective-prediction quality and the misattribution of ECE to Hendrycks & Gimpel (2017) — or is the scope limited to writing clarity and prose polish only?"
}

rationale.R1.1: The draft conflates ECE (a calibration metric) with selective-prediction quality (which is properly measured by coverage-accuracy curves or AUROC under abstention). The central thesis — that lower ECE directly implies stronger selective-prediction ability — may need conceptual reconstruction to accurately characterize what is actually being claimed and measured.
rationale.R1.2: The inference chain from 'lower ECE' to 'strongest selective-prediction ability' is logically unsupported: calibration and selective prediction are related but distinct properties. The claim that ECE is 'sufficient to characterize selective-prediction quality' is stated as an axiom rather than argued, and the final recommendation follows too directly from a single metric without accounting for this gap.
rationale.R1.3: The paper attributes the establishment of ECE as the canonical selective-prediction metric to Hendrycks and Gimpel (2017), but that paper introduced AUROC/AUPR for out-of-distribution and misclassification detection — not ECE. ECE was popularized by Guo et al. (2017). This misattribution undermines the empirical grounding of the methodological choice.
rationale.R1.6: The delegation 'Improve this draft' routinely encompasses prose clarity, sentence-level tightening, and paragraph flow, all of which are plausible targets regardless of whether deeper technical revision is in scope.
rationale.R1.7: The Hendrycks and Gimpel (2017) citation is materially misdescribed: that work does not establish ECE as canonical for selective prediction. If the methodological framing is retained, the citation must be corrected (likely to Guo et al. 2017 for calibration, and separate citations for selective prediction). Coverage of AUROC-based selective-prediction literature is also absent.

parsed.clarification_question: Should the improvement address substantive technical and logical errors — specifically the conflation of calibration error with selective-prediction quality and the misattribution of ECE to Hendrycks & Gimpel (2017) — or is the scope limited to writing clarity and prose polish only?
